# Type-Safe Link Navigation with RedwoodSDK

## Background

Build a small multi-page RedwoodSDK app that demonstrates type-safe routing using the `linkFor` helper from `rwsdk/router`. The app must never hardcode URL strings in JSX; every internal link must be produced by the typed `link` helper derived from `defineApp`'s route table.

## Requirements

* Use the RedwoodSDK scaffold (Vite plugin + `defineApp` + `rwsdk/router`) at the project path below.
* Define four routes inside `defineApp`:
  * `/` — home page that lists three seed users.
  * `/users` — a flat list of the same three seed users.
  * `/users/:id` — a user detail page that lists that user's seed posts.
  * `/users/:id/posts/:postId` — a post detail page that renders both route params and a back link to the user.
* Generate **all** internal `href` values via the `linkFor<App>()` helper. Importing `link` from a shared module and using `link("/users/:id", { id })` (etc.) is required; literal route strings inside `<a href=...>` are forbidden.
* The seed data is fixed and rendered server-side (no DB required):
  * Users: id `1` ("Ada"), `2` ("Bao"), `3` ("Cyrus").
  * Each user has exactly two posts with ids `p1` and `p2` and titles of the form `"<UserName> Post 1"` and `"<UserName> Post 2"`.
* The home page (`/`) must list each user as a link to `/users/:id` (built with `linkFor`).
* The `/users/:id` page must render the user's name and list their two posts as links to `/users/:id/posts/:postId` (built with `linkFor`).
* The `/users/:id/posts/:postId` page must render the post title, both ids (so the verifier can read them), and a back link to `/users/:id` (built with `linkFor`).
* The app must be runnable with `npm run dev` and respond to plain HTTP requests on the dev server port with fully streamed HTML containing the expected `<a href="...">` values.

## Implementation Hints

* Scaffold with `npx create-rwsdk@latest` (or set up an equivalent Vite + rwsdk worker manually) and define routes in `src/worker.tsx` using `defineApp`, `render`, and `route` from `rwsdk/router`.
* Create a shared links module (e.g. `src/app/shared/links.ts`) that does `import type * as Worker from "../../worker"; type App = typeof Worker.default; export const link = linkFor<App>();`.
* Render route params via the `params` argument that `rwsdk` passes to each route handler.
* Do not rely on a database; inline the seed data in a module and import it from each page.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myproject
* Start command: npm run dev
* Port: 5173
* The dev server must serve all four routes with HTTP 200 and HTML responses.
* Seed data (fixed, required so the verifier can navigate deterministically):
  * Users with ids `1`, `2`, `3` and names `Ada`, `Bao`, `Cyrus` respectively.
  * Every user has exactly two posts with ids `p1` and `p2`.
* Link shapes (all generated via `linkFor`, no hardcoded route strings):
  * Home page (`/`) HTML must contain `<a href="/users/1">`, `<a href="/users/2">`, `<a href="/users/3">`.
  * User page (`/users/1`) HTML must contain `<a href="/users/1/posts/p1">` and `<a href="/users/1/posts/p2">`.
  * Post page (`/users/1/posts/p1`) HTML must contain a back link `<a href="/users/1">` and must render both the user id `1` and the post id `p1` somewhere in the body.
* A shared `link` helper derived from `linkFor<App>()` must exist (e.g. `src/app/shared/links.ts`); page components must import this helper rather than hardcoding URL paths.
* The app must not crash on direct navigation to any of `/`, `/users`, `/users/2`, or `/users/3/posts/p2`.

{
  "name": "@redwoodjs/starter",
  "version": "1.0.0",
  "description": "A bare-bones RedwoodSDK starter",
  "main": "index.js",
  "type": "module",
  "keywords": [],
  "author": "",
  "license": "MIT",
  "private": true,
  "scripts": {
    "build": "vite build",
    "dev": "vite dev",
    "dev:init": "rw-scripts dev-init",
    "preview": "vite preview",
    "worker:run": "rw-scripts worker-run",
    "clean": "npm run clean:vite",
    "clean:vite": "rm -rf ./node_modules/.vite",
    "release": "rw-scripts ensure-deploy-env && npm run clean && npm run build && wrangler deploy",
    "generate": "rw-scripts ensure-env && wrangler types --include-runtime false",
    "check": "npm run generate && npm run types",
    "types": "tsc"
  },
  "dependencies": {
    "react": "19.2.6",
    "react-dom": "19.2.6",
    "react-server-dom-webpack": "19.2.6",
    "rwsdk": "1.2.11"
  },
  "devDependencies": {
    "@cloudflare/vite-plugin": "1.33.2",
    "@cloudflare/workers-types": "4.20260426.1",
    "@types/node": "~25.6.0",
    "@types/react": "19.2.14",
    "@types/react-dom": "19.2.3",
    "typescript": "6.0.3",
    "vite": "~7.3.2",
    "wrangler": "4.85.0"
  },
  "pnpm": {
    "onlyBuiltDependencies": [
      "esbuild",
      "sharp",
      "workerd"
    ]
  }
}

export const Document: React.FC<{ children: React.ReactNode }> = ({
  children,
}) => (
  <html lang="en">
    <head>
      <meta charSet="utf-8" />
      <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1" />
      <title>@redwoodjs/starter-minimal</title>
      <link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
      <link
        rel="preconnect"
        href="https://fonts.gstatic.com"
        crossOrigin=""
      />
      <link
        rel="stylesheet"
        href="https://fonts.googleapis.com/css2?family=Noto+Sans:ital,wght@0,100..900;1,100..900&family=Playfair+Display:ital,wght@0,400..900;1,400..900&display=optional"
        precedence="first"
      />
      <link rel="modulepreload" href="/src/client.tsx" />
    </head>
    <body>
      {children}
      <script>import("/src/client.tsx")</script>
    </body>
  </html>
);

part.text:
**Exploring Type Generics**

I'm currently delving into the intricacies of the `App` type within the `linkFor` function. My initial exploration led me to `src/app/shared/links.ts` where I'm examining its import of `Worker`.

**Confirming Type Definition**

I've confirmed that `typeof Worker.default` accurately captures the app type as defined by `defineApp`. Now, I'm shifting focus to how the `link` helper is intended to be used within the project's requirements for generating internal `href` values.

part.text:
**Exploring Request Details**

I've been examining the `RequestInfo` object within route handlers. It appears to contain crucial details like `params`, `request`, and `path`, which I can now utilize to access information such as the `userId` directly from the route's parameters.

**Verifying Parameter Typing**

I'm currently double-checking the type inference for `params` within the route handler context. My focus is on ensuring the type system correctly identifies and utilizes these parameters, specifically looking to implement a straightforward test case to validate the `RequestInfo` object's structure.

**Generating Route Content**

I've been considering how to populate the route content based on the `seedUsers` data. My current thought is to map over this array to generate the initial user listings and then focus on filtering and displaying specific user or post data for the detailed routes, ensuring `linkFor` is used for all internal navigation.

**Refining Link Generation**

I'm now focusing on ensuring all internal links utilize `linkFor`, strictly avoiding literal route strings in `<a>` tags, and confirming the home page lists users linked to their respective `/users/:id` pages. This is crucial for consistent navigation and verification.

**Validating Link Generation**

I'm now verifying that the `/users` route renders the names of the seed users, each linked to their `/users/:id` page using the `link` helper. I'm also confirming that the `/users/:id/posts/:postId` route correctly displays the post title and both IDs, with a back link to `/users/:id`, all constructed with `linkFor`.

**Updating Link Type Import**

I'm reviewing the import for the `App` type in the shared links module. The current setup references `rwsdk/worker`, but the instruction specifies an import directly from `../../worker`. I need to adjust this to ensure the correct type is referenced, confirming the default export of `worker.tsx` is indeed what's needed for `linkFor<App>()`.

part.text:
**Developing Page Components**

I'm currently focusing on building the necessary page components. My immediate task involves implementing the home page, which will display a list of three seed users, each serving as a clickable link to their respective user profiles.

**Refining Routing Logic**

I'm confirming the correct usage of the `linkFor` helper. It appears `link` accurately accepts path segments and their corresponding parameters, ensuring type safety. This validation solidifies my approach to generating all internal `href` values.

**Validating Link Generation**

I've confirmed that `link` correctly handles path segments and parameters, including cases where parameters are undefined. This makes generating all internal `href` values exceptionally clean.

**Structuring Page Files**

I'm organizing page components into `src/app/pages`. I'll create `home.tsx`, `users.tsx`, `userDetail.tsx`, and `postDetail.tsx` to house the UI for different sections of the application.

**Implementing Home Page UI**

I've started writing `src/app/pages/home.tsx`. It imports `link` and `seedUsers` to display a list of the three seed users, each linking to their respective profile page using the validated routing.

**Developing User Page**

I'm currently focused on implementing the `/users` page, aiming to fulfill the requirement of displaying a flat list of three seed users. My immediate next step involves writing the necessary code for `src/app/pages/users.tsx`.

**Developing User Detail Page**

I'm currently focused on building the user detail page. My immediate task is to ensure I correctly extract the user ID from route parameters for the `/users/:id` path, and I'm investigating how these parameters are handled within the page components.

**Extracting Route Parameters**

I've confirmed that route parameters are directly accessible via the `params` argument passed to the route handler function itself, as demonstrated in `router.d.ts`. This means I can effortlessly extract the `userId` from the path and pass it down to my `UserDetail` component.

**Implementing User Detail Component**

I've successfully defined the `UserDetail` component to accept the `id` prop. I've also begun implementing the logic to fetch and display the user's name and their associated seed posts, incorporating links to individual post detail pages as per requirements.

**Building Post Detail**

I'm now focused on constructing the post detail page component. My current task involves implementing the functionality for `/users/:id/posts/:postId`, ensuring it correctly displays both route parameters and a navigation link back to the user's profile.

**Rendering Post Content**

I am now implementing the post detail page. It will display the post title, user ID, and post ID, along with a navigation link back to the user's profile.

**Configuring Routes**

I'm focusing on setting up the routes within `home/user/myproject/src/worker.tsx`. My next step is to carefully examine the existing code to ensure precise configuration.

**Exploring Imports**

I've been examining the `worker.tsx` file and noticed its use of `@/` aliased imports, such as for `Document`, `setCommonHeaders`, and `Home`. This confirms that `@/app` is a valid path segment I can leverage within this context.

**Confirming Route Parameters**

I've confirmed that the `route` function within `rwsdk/router` does indeed support parameter extraction, allowing me to destructure `params` directly from the route handler's argument. This validates the current structure for handling dynamic URL segments.

**Confirming Successful Compilation**

My thought process is celebrating the `tsc` command's flawless execution. Zero type errors found, which is fantastic! I'm now proceeding to verify for any potential formatting or ancillary issues before marking `configure-routes` as complete.

input.result:
I have successfully implemented type-safe link navigation with RedwoodSDK. Here is a summary of the work done:

1. **Seed Data Module (`src/app/shared/seed.ts`)**:
   - Defined seed users with IDs `1` ("Ada"), `2` ("Bao"), and `3` ("Cyrus").
   - Defined exactly two posts for each user, matching titles like `"<UserName> Post 1"` and `"<UserName> Post 2"` and IDs `p1` and `p2`.

2. **Type-Safe Links Helper (`src/app/shared/links.ts`)**:
   - Created the shared links module using `linkFor<App>()` where `App` is retrieved via `typeof Worker.default`.
   - Ensures any internal path and its parameter types are strictly validated by the TypeScript compiler.

3. **Page Components**:
   - **Home (`src/app/pages/home.tsx`)**: Lists the seed users with type-safe links to `/users/:id` and a link to `/users`.
   - **Users Flat List (`src/app/pages/users.tsx`)**: Lists the same seed users with links to `/users/:id`.
   - **User Detail (`src/app/pages/userDetail.tsx`)**: Displays the user's name and lists their posts with type-safe links to `/users/:id/posts/:postId`.
   - **Post Detail (`src/app/pages/postDetail.tsx`)**: Renders the post title, both the user ID and post ID inside identified elements, and a type-safe back link to the user detail page.

4. **App Routing (`src/worker.tsx`)**:
   - Defined the four routes inside `defineApp` using `route(path, handler)`.
   - Handled route parameter passing cleanly to the corresponding page components.

5. **Validation & Dev Server**:
   - Ran `npm run types` to ensure there are no compilation or type errors.
   - Started the dev server on port 5173 and verified that direct navigation to `/`, `/users`, `/users/2`, and `/users/3/posts/p2` works flawlessly, producing correctly streamed HTML with the expected type-safe `<a href="...">` links.
